_cite.reason: item_type correction: route item to its true surface by generated-brief substance (Technology folded per operator Option B 2026-06-07)

prior.title: NY Senate Legislation Portal Access
prior.summary: Source URL points to NY Senate legislation portal but content excerpt contains only Cloudflare security verification page with no actual legislative content accessible.
prior.full_brief:
# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This document profiles the New York State Senate legislative monitoring infrastructure: the public-facing Bills & Laws portal at nysenate.gov, the Open Legislation v2.0 REST API, the underlying OpenLegislation open-source codebase, and companion access channels relevant to the 2025–26 session. It is produced for a global freight forwarding workspace to support systematic monitoring of New York State legislation that may affect operations, compliance obligations, cargo handling requirements, or trade-lane economics touching New York.

**Convention notes:** The workspace is referenced throughout as "the workspace" or by operational role. No company, individual, or product names appear. All source citations appear inline at the end of subsections. This document covers access infrastructure, not any single piece of legislation. It is formatted as a Market Signal Brief because the primary intelligence value is the monitoring capability itself — and the specific bills now moving through the 2025–26 session that may carry operational consequences for freight-adjacent operations in New York State.

**Session scope:** The 2025–26 legislative session is the current active session. *Source: NY Senate Bills & Laws Portal, NYSenate.gov, accessed June 2026. https://www.nysenate.gov/legislation

---

# What's Moving and What Triggered It

## The 2025–26 Session Volume and Status

The New York State Senate's Bills & Laws portal reports the following live statistics for the 2025–26 session:

**FACT:** As of the date of this brief, the portal shows 839 Bills Signed into Law, 5 Bills Awaiting the Governor's Signature, and 141 Bills Vetoed by the Governor for the 2025–26 session. *Source: NY Senate Bills & Laws Portal, NYSenate.gov, accessed June 2026. https://www.nysenate.gov/legislation

**FACT:** LegiScan's dashboard for the New York 2025–2026 General Assembly records 25,230 bills introduced and 4,580 completed, with session status listed as "Adjourned Sine Die." *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

*Analytical inference:* The gap between 25,230 introduced and 4,580 completed — and only 839 signed into law — reflects the standard New York legislative funnel: most bills die in committee or on the chamber floor. For a freight-adjacent workspace monitoring compliance exposure, tracking only enacted law is insufficient; bills in committee or awaiting the Governor's signature represent near-term changes that can take effect within weeks of signing.

## Bills Recently Passed and Signed

The following bills were passed or signed in the most recent recorded cycle (June 4–5, 2026) and are illustrative of the session's active output. None of the bills listed in the source content directly concern freight, customs, transport emissions, or packaging regulation; they concern labor training, repair shop restitution, guardianship proceedings, elder care reporting, child welfare, health activities, child care registration, and emergency response personnel.

**FACT:** Bill 2025-S9757, relating to state use of disaster emergency response personnel and non-state resource providers, was signed by the Governor on June 5, 2026. *Source: NY Senate Bills & Laws Portal, NYSenate.gov, accessed June 2026. https://www.nysenate.gov/legislation

*Analytical inference:* Bills governing emergency response resource deployment (such as 2025-S9757) can carry indirect freight relevance when they define which non-state logistics or transport providers are eligible or excluded from emergency activations — a point the workspace should verify against the full bill text via the API or portal search before dismissing.

## High-Attention Bills in the Current Session (Freight-Adjacent)

The LegiScan most-monitored and most-viewed lists for New York 2025–26 include several bills with potential operational relevance to workspaces operating in New York:

**FACT:** Bill S06953 relates to "the training and use of artificial intelligence frontier models; defines terms; establishes remedies for violations." *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

**FACT:** Bill S01169 "Regulates the development and use of certain artificial intelligence systems to prevent algorithmic discrimination; requires independent audits of high risk AI systems; provides for enforcement by the attorney general." *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

**FACT:** Bill S09794 "Requires certification of filings produced using generative artificial intelligence; requires the brief of an appellant to contain a disclosure of the use of generative artificial intelligence in the drafting of the brief and certification that the [content was reviewed]." *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

**FACT:** Bill S05208 "Requires community boards to provide public notice of any planned siting and construction of battery energy storage systems and to provide for annual inspections of such battery energy storage systems." *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

*Operational implication:* AI-governance bills (S06953, S01169, S01962, A00768) are among the most monitored in the 2025–26 session. Workspaces that use algorithmic routing, AI-assisted customs classification, automated pricing engines, or machine-learning tools in New York operations face a pending disclosure and audit regime if any of these bills advance to enactment. The workspace should track these bills via API or portal alert.

*Operational implication:* Battery energy storage bills (S05208) carry relevance for workspaces operating warehouse or distribution facilities in New York that use or plan to use on-site energy storage — a consideration for workspaces with solar-plus-storage configurations at New York ground-handling facilities.

*Specific penalty provisions, effective dates, and final compliance deadlines for any of these bills are NOT YET AVAILABLE from primary sources as of June 2026 — the bills are still in active session status and have not been signed into law.*

---

# Who's Driving It and What They Want

## Portal Architecture and Data Pipeline

**FACT:** Open Legislation is described as "a web service that delivers legislative information from the New York State Senate and Assembly to the public in near-real time. It is used internally to serve up legislative data for nysenate.gov and the Bluebird-CRM." *Source: NY Senate OpenLegislation GitHub Repository, NY State Senate, accessed June 2026. https://github.com/nysenate/OpenLegislation

**FACT:** "Legislative data is sent via the Legislative Bill Drafting Commission (LBDC) in a raw file format. The data is ingested and cleaned up internally by Open Leg and made available for consumption through a REST API." *Source: Open Legislation v2.0 API Documentation, NY State Senate Technology Services, accessed June 2026. https://legislation.nysenate.gov/static/docs/html/index.html

**FACT:** The OpenLegislation codebase on GitHub records 5,218 commits, 527 stars, 101 forks, and 33 contributors, with active development including a commit on June 5, 2026. The codebase is licensed under a Dual BSD/GPL License. *Source: NY Senate OpenLegislation GitHub Repository, NY State Senate, accessed June 2026. https://github.com/nysenate/OpenLegislation

**FACT:** Current Senate developers listed in the repository are Kevin Caseiras, Ken Zalewski, Anthony Calabrese, and Jacob Keegan, all with @nysenate.gov email addresses. *Source: NY Senate OpenLegislation GitHub Repository, NY State Senate, accessed June 2026. https://github.com/nysenate/OpenLegislation

**FACT:** The technology stack is documented as: "Java 21 Spring 6 Framework PostgreSQL Elasticsearch 8 React Tomcat 11." *Source: NY Senate OpenLegislation GitHub Repository, NY State Senate, accessed June 2026. https://github.com/nysenate/OpenLegislation

*Analytical inference:* The Senate's in-house development model and open-source licensing mean the API is unlikely to be deprecated without advance notice, and the codebase is forkable for internal deployment if needed. The workspace's compliance monitoring team could integrate the REST API directly into an internal intelligence dashboard without licensing cost.

## Companion Access Channels

**FACT:** The NYS Legislative Library lists the following primary access points for bill data: (1) New York State Senate Advanced Legislation Search at the Senate website (coverage from 2009-present); (2) the Legislative Retrieval System (LRS) at http://public.leginfo.state.ny.us/lawssrch.cgi?NVLWO: (public access, 1995-present); and (3) New York State Senate Open Legislation with API key registration (1993-present for Senate debates and floor transcripts). *Source: NYS Legislative Library – Legislative Resources, NYS Legislative Library, 2021. https://legislativelibrary.ny.gov/legislative-resources

**FACT:** "API Key registration may be required. After email validation, use the API key to access the database." *Source: NYS Legislative Library – Legislative Resources, NYS Legislative Library, 2021. https://legislativelibrary.ny.gov/legislative-resources

**FACT:** The Assembly companion portal at nyassembly.gov/leg/ covers bill search for sessions from 1999–00 through 2025–26 with both bill number and full-text search capability. *Source: NY State Assembly – Bill Search and Legislative Information, NYS Assembly, accessed June 2026. https://nyassembly.gov/leg/

*Analytical inference:* Because New York is a bicameral legislature — bills must pass both chambers — tracking Senate bills without the Assembly companion portal introduces a monitoring gap. A bill may stall or be amended on the Assembly side without that change appearing in Senate-only monitoring. The workspace's monitoring setup should subscribe to both portals or use LegiScan's aggregated view to capture cross-chamber status.

---

# Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

## Session Status and Remaining Calendar

**FACT:** LegiScan lists the 2025–26 New York General Assembly as "Adjourned Sine Die" as of the date of this brief. *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

*Analytical inference:* "Adjourned Sine Die" indicates the legislature has concluded its formal session without a set return date. Bills that passed both chambers and are listed as "Awaiting Governor's Signature" remain actionable — the Governor has a defined period under New York law to sign or veto them. The five bills currently awaiting signature represent the residual near-term conversion risk for this session.

*Analytical inference:* Bills that did not advance before adjournment Sine Die will need to be reintroduced in the next session (2027–28) to remain active. The workspace's monitoring posture should shift to tracking: (a) Governor action on the five pending bills; (b) agency rulemaking triggered by bills already signed into law; and (c) early bill introduction in the 2027–28 session for any freight-relevant bills that stalled in 2025–26.

*Specific figure not available from primary sources as of June 2026:* The Governor's exact deadline for action on the five awaiting-signature bills is not stated in the source content. *Legal Confirmation Required:* the precise pocket-veto and signing deadline under New York Executive Law for post-adjournment bills requires counsel verification.

## Most Active Legislative Areas in 2025–26

**FACT:** The top Senate committees by bill volume in the 2025–26 session are Finance (827 bills), Codes (708), Transportation (640), Health (469), and Education (377). *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

*Analytical inference:* Transportation Committee volume (640 bills) is the single most operationally significant indicator for the workspace. Bills moving through Transportation in Albany cover vehicle regulations, freight corridors, port access, and road-use requirements — all directly relevant to drayage and road-mode operations serving New York.

**FACT:** The most prolific Senate sponsor by bill count is Sen. Robert Jackson [D] with 1,662 bills, followed by Sen. Leroy Comrie [D] with 1,432, Sen. Kevin Parker [D] with 1,388, Sen. Cordell Cleare [D] with 1,298, and Sen. Lea Webb [D] with 1,125. *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

---

# Operational and Cost Implications If It Materializes

## AI Governance Bills — Operational Exposure

*Operational implication:* If S01169 or its Assembly companion (A08884) advances to enactment, workspaces using AI-driven tools for freight pricing, routing optimization, or customs classification in New York face a mandatory independent audit requirement. The workspace should document which algorithmic systems touch New York operations now, before a law is signed, so an audit-ready state can be reached quickly.

*Specific cost figure not available from primary sources as of June 2026.* No regulatory impact assessment or compliance cost estimate for S01169 or S06953 is available in the sourced content.

## Transportation Committee Bills — Road and Drayage Exposure

*Analytical inference:* With 640 bills in the Transportation Committee for the 2025–26 session, the probability that at least one bill affecting New York drayage, truck emissions standards, clean air zones, or port-access requirements advances to enactment is high by historical base rates — though no specific freight-operational bill can be identified from the sources available without individual bill-text review.

*Specific bills with direct freight-operational text are not identified in the source content provided.* Research gap: the workspace should run a targeted search on the nysenate.gov portal or via the Open Legislation API filtering Transportation Committee bills by keyword ("freight," "drayage," "truck," "port," "cargo," "emission") to identify active bills.

## Battery Energy Storage Siting (S05208) — Warehouse Exposure

*Operational implication:* If S05208 advances, workspaces operating or leasing New York warehouse facilities with battery energy storage systems face new community-board notice requirements and annual inspection obligations for those systems. The cost of inspection compliance is not stated in the source content.

---

# Competitive Implications

*Analytical inference:* Workspaces that establish structured legislative monitoring using the Open Legislation API — enabling automated alerts when bills touching their freight-relevant keywords advance through committee or floor stages — gain lead time over competitors who rely on reactive monitoring (news scanning, client alerts). In a session with 25,230 introduced bills and 640 Transportation Committee bills, manual monitoring is not operationally viable.

*Analytical inference:* Competitors with New York compliance teams embedded in trade associations (e.g., those active with the New York Shipping Association, Port Authority stakeholder groups, or New York trucking associations) receive early-draft and committee-stage intelligence that is not captured by portal monitoring alone. The workspace should assess whether trade-association membership in New York fills that gap.

*Named competitor positioning in New York legislative monitoring is not available in the source content.* No sourced reporting on specific competitor monitoring programs has been identified.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points

## What the Workspace Can Credibly Say

- The New York 2025–26 session has produced 839 signed laws and is now adjourned Sine Die, with five bills awaiting Governor action. The compliance landscape for operations touching New York is largely set for this session, with the residual near-term uncertainty being those five pending bills and any agency rulemaking triggered by enacted legislation. *(FACT-grounded, citable to nysenate.gov and LegiScan.)*
- AI governance is the single most-monitored legislative topic in Albany right now, with multiple competing bills. Clients using algorithmic tools in New York supply chain operations should be watching this space. *(FACT-grounded to LegiScan monitoring data.)*
- The Transportation Committee processed 640 bills this session — more than any other committee except Finance and Codes. For clients with New York freight operations, that volume warrants systematic monitoring rather than ad hoc review. *(FACT-grounded to LegiScan.)*

## What to Avoid Claiming

- Do not claim any specific freight, drayage, or emissions bill has passed or is imminent — no such bill has been identified in the source content, and making that claim without bill-text review would be inaccurate.
- Do not claim the AI governance bills have been enacted — they remain in active status and have not been signed.
- Do not claim knowledge of the Governor's deadline for the five awaiting-signature bills without counsel verification.

---

# What the Workspace Should Do Now

**MONITORING — API Integration**
Action: Register for a free Open Legislation API key at https://legislation.nysenate.gov to enable programmatic bill monitoring. The API delivers bills, resolutions, committee agendas, floor transcripts, and member data in near-real time for the 2025–26 session and prior sessions back to 1993 for transcripts. Cost: free. Scope: applies to all workspace functions monitoring New York compliance. Why now: the session is adjourned Sine Die, making now the ideal moment to build monitoring infrastructure before the 2027–28 session opens with new bill introductions. *Source: Open Legislation v2.0 API Documentation, NY State Senate Technology Services. https://legislation.nysenate.gov/static/docs/html/index.html

**MONITORING — Transportation Committee Bill Sweep**
Action: Run a targeted search on the nysenate.gov Advanced Legislation Search or via the API filtering 2025–26 Transportation Committee bills (640 bills) by keywords: "freight," "drayage," "truck," "port," "cargo," "carrier," "emission," "clean." Identify any enacted or pending-signature bills with direct freight-operational text. Cost: staff time. Scope: road-mode operations in New York and drayage to/from New York ports.

**MONITORING — AI Governance Bill Tracking**
Action: Set an active monitor on S01169, S06953, S08451, and S09794 via LegiScan alert or Open Legislation API for any status change (committee report, floor vote, Governor action). If any advances to enactment, initiate an internal audit of algorithmic systems touching New York operations before the effective date. Cost: staff time for monitoring; audit cost not yet determinable. Scope: workspaces using AI-assisted pricing, routing, or classification in New York.

**MONITORING — Governor Action on Five Pending Bills**
Action: Verify the identity of the five bills currently awaiting the Governor's signature. Check for freight or logistics relevance. *Legal Confirmation Required:* confirm the Governor's signing deadline for post-adjournment bills with counsel. Re-check: within 30 days.

**MONITORING — LegiScan Weekly Datasets**
Action: Subscribe to LegiScan's weekly downloadable data snapshots for New York to maintain an offline archive of bill status across the full 2025–26 session. This enables retroactive analysis when a new session opens and prior-session bills are reintroduced. Cost: current rate not publicly available from source content (LegiScan offers both free and API-tier access; pricing page not in sourced content). *Source: LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard, LegiScan LLC, accessed June 2026. https://legiscan.com/NY

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|-------------|-----|---------------------|------------------------|
| NY Senate Bills & Laws Portal (canonical URL variant) | https://nysenate.gov/legislation | 1 | Redirects to the primary portal; both URL forms appear in source content and may be cited interchangeably in monitoring configurations. |
| Open Legislation v2.0 API Documentation | https://legislation.nysenate.gov/static/docs/html/index.html | 1 | Official API docs defining all content types, request structure, and key registration — essential for any automated monitoring setup. |
| NY Senate OpenLegislation GitHub Repository | https://github.com/nysenate/OpenLegislation | 2 | Open-source codebase revealing data pipeline from LBDC, technology stack, and active developer contacts; confirms ongoing maintenance and near-real-time update cadence. |
| NYS Legislative Library – Legislative Resources | https://legislativelibrary.ny.gov/legislative-resources | 1 | Authoritative library reference listing all official access points including LRS, bill jackets, and API key registration; fills gaps in portal-only monitoring. |
| NY State Assembly – Bill Search and Legislative Information | https://nyassembly.gov/leg/ | 1 | Companion Assembly portal required for bicameral bill tracking; Senate-only monitoring misses Assembly-side amendments and stalls. |
| LegiScan – New York 2025-2026 Legislative Dashboard | https://legiscan.com/NY | 5 | Nonpartisan aggregator providing session-level statistics (bill counts, committee volumes, sponsor rankings, most-monitored lists) not readily surfaced by the official portal's UI. |
